questions.q: What was Anaconda Inc. originally called?
questions.why: Anaconda Inc. was originally Continuum Analytics. Conda was derived and created from Continuum members.

questions.q: "Be the kitten" — what does this internal Anaconda phrase mean?
options: Approach change with curiosity instead of fear
A code name for a stealth product
questions.why: From the April 2022 All-Hands — Peter talked about change in the context of fear vs. curiosity. The kitten shows up at 8:55 in the recording.

questions.q: Which Jira project does the Security team use for active sprint work (not the queue)?
questions.why: AEGIS is the Security Team board. SOTERIA is the Security Now queue for unparented incoming items.

questions.q: Which security tool is Anaconda *replacing* with Socket.dev for supply-chain scanning?
questions.why: Per the Security Tools Ownership Register: Socket.dev is replacing Snyk. Snyk is retained for historical reference.

questions.q: Which is the Vulnerability Disclosure Program / bug bounty platform Anaconda uses?
questions.why: Intigriti runs Anaconda's VDP / bug bounty. Cobalt is the separate pentest vendor.

questions.q: The first computer bug was a literal bug. What was it?
options: A spider on a vacuum tube
A moth in a relay of the Harvard Mark II
A cricket in an ENIAC fan
questions.why: September 9, 1947 — Grace Hopper's team taped a moth into the Mark II logbook with the note 'first actual case of bug being found.'

questions.q: What does CVE stand for?
questions.why: Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures, registered with the NVD at NIST. Anaconda's package team curates them for repos.

questions.q: What category of attack is Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228)?
options: Remote Code Execution via JNDI lookup
questions.why: RCE via JNDI lookup in Log4j — a single string like ${jndi:ldap://...} in a logged field popped half the internet.

questions.q: The 2024 XZ Utils backdoor (CVE-2024-3094) was inserted by an attacker using what alias?
questions.why: Jia Tan spent ~2 years building maintainer trust before slipping a backdoor into liblzma. Andres Freund caught it from a 500ms SSH delay.

questions.q: What does the OWASP Top 10 rank?
options: The most critical web application security risks
The top 10 worst CISOs of the year
questions.why: OWASP Top 10 — most critical web app security risks. Currently A01: Broken Access Control sits at #1.

questions.q: Heartbleed exploited a buffer over-read in which library?
questions.why: OpenSSL — a missing bounds check in the TLS heartbeat extension leaked up to 64KB of server memory per request. Disclosed April 2014.

questions.q: What's the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption (in one phrase)?
options: Symmetric is faster but less secure
Symmetric uses one shared key; asymmetric uses a key pair (public/private)
Asymmetric only works on text, symmetric works on binary
Symmetric is for storage, asymmetric for transit
questions.why: Symmetric = one shared secret (AES). Asymmetric = key pair (RSA, ECC). TLS uses asymmetric to exchange a symmetric key, then symmetric for the bulk traffic.

questions.q: Who released the Morris Worm in 1988, accidentally taking down ~10% of the internet?
options: Robert Tappan Morris (a Cornell grad student)
questions.why: Robert Tappan Morris, a Cornell grad student, was the first person convicted under the U.S. Computer Fraud and Abuse Act. He's now an MIT professor.

questions.q: In the CIA triad, what does the 'I' stand for?
questions.why: Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability — the foundational triad. Integrity = data hasn't been tampered with.

questions.q: What is the actual purpose of HTTP status code 418?
options: A joke from RFC 2324: "I'm a teapot"
questions.why: RFC 2324 — Hyper Text Coffee Pot Control Protocol (HTCPCP). An April Fools' RFC from 1998. "I'm a teapot" means the server refuses to brew coffee because it is a teapot.

questions.q: In Hackers (1995), what's Dade Murphy's hacker handle?
options: Both Crash Override AND Zero Cool
questions.why: Dade is Zero Cool as a kid (the 1988 stock market crash) and goes by Crash Override after his ban expires. Hack the planet!

questions.q: In The Matrix (1999), what real CLI tool does Trinity actually use to break into the power grid?
options: nmap (with a real CVE: SSH1 CRC32 exploit)
She made it up — it was prop dialogue
questions.why: Trinity runs nmap against a host, finds SSH1, and exploits CVE-2001-0144 (the SSHv1 CRC32 compensation attack). One of the most technically accurate hacking scenes ever filmed.

questions.q: Mr. Robot protagonist Elliot Alderson works at a fictional cybersecurity firm called what?
questions.why: AllSafe protects E Corp (Evil Corp) — the company Elliot's hacktivist group fsociety is trying to take down. Steel Mountain is the data backup facility they target.

questions.q: James Bond's gadget-supplying quartermaster is known as what single letter?
questions.why: Q, head of Q Branch (Quartermaster). Played most famously by Desmond Llewelyn (17 films) and now Ben Whishaw. M is Bond's boss.

questions.q: In Sneakers (1992), what does the magic decryption box actually break?
options: Any cryptographic system (it's a black-box that solves discrete log)
questions.why: The MacGuffin chip in Sneakers is presented as breaking any cryptosystem based on discrete logarithm or factoring — i.e., it kills RSA, DH, DSA, ECDSA all at once. Basically a fictional quantum computer, 25 years early.

questions.q: Which spy franchise gave us the line: 'I'm getting too old for this sh*t'?
questions.why: Roger Murtaugh in Lethal Weapon. Not technically a spy movie but firmly in the action canon — said in every single one of the four films.

questions.q: In WarGames (1983), what's the name of the WOPR's password-cracking AI personality?
questions.why: Joshua — the password is the name of Falken's deceased son. The famous final line: 'A strange game. The only winning move is not to play.'

questions.q: Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy centers on hunting which kind of spy inside MI6?
questions.why: Smiley is brought out of retirement to find a mole at the top of the Circus, planted by Karla. Le Carré coined modern spy-thriller vocabulary: mole, honeytrap, lamplighter, scalphunter.

questions.q: In Mission: Impossible (1996), what does Ethan Hunt suspend himself from to avoid touching the floor?
options: A skylight cable in the CIA Langley vault
A grappling hook in a Kremlin hallway
A drone in the Burj Khalifa
questions.why: The Langley NOC list heist. Pressure-sensitive floor, temperature-sensitive ceiling, sound-sensitive room. The single bead of sweat about to drop is one of cinema's great suspense beats.

questions.q: Which real cipher gets cracked at Bletchley Park in The Imitation Game (2014)?
questions.why: Turing's bombe machine is built to break Enigma. (Fun corollary: the higher-level Lorenz cipher used by German High Command was broken by Tommy Flowers' Colossus, the first electronic computer — but that's a different movie that hasn't been made yet.)

questions.q: What's Jason Bourne's real name in The Bourne Identity?
questions.why: David Webb. 'Jason Bourne' is the codename for his Treadstone-program operative identity. The four aliases listed are all actual covers he used in the films.

questions.q: In Catch Me If You Can, what professions does Frank Abagnale impersonate?
questions.why: Pan Am pilot, ER doctor, Louisiana lawyer — all by his early 20s, all without credentials. Based on Abagnale's (somewhat embellished) memoir. Caught by FBI agent Carl Hanratty (a composite character).

questions.q: In Skyfall, what's the name of Bond's family estate in Scotland?
questions.why: Just Skyfall — engraved on the gate. The film's climax is staged there as Bond and M defend it against Silva. Iconic Sam Mendes shot of the house silhouetted against the moor at dusk.

questions.q: In Inception, what object does Cobb spin to test if he's in a dream?
questions.why: A spinning top — if it falls, he's awake; if it spins forever, he's dreaming. The final shot of the movie is the top wobbling — Nolan refused to confirm whether it falls.

questions.q: In Snowden (and real life), what NSA program was leaked, revealing mass collection of phone metadata?
questions.why: STELLARWIND / Section 215 was the bulk metadata program. PRISM (different program, also leaked) collected internet content from major providers. Both made the front pages in June 2013.
